title: Precocious Puberty: Central (GDPP) vs Peripheral (GIPP) Etiology
subtitle: Mapping the node of failure — gonadotropin-dependent vs gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty (Pediatric Endocrinology)

items.title: How GDPP and GIPP differ at the node of failure
items.content: The central-versus-peripheral split is about where the pathology sits in the endocrine architecture. Gonadotropin-dependent precocious puberty (GDPP, central) is a premature reactivation of the hypothalamic GnRH pulse generator: the HPG axis is intact but switched into a mature operational state too early — so it is a loss of central inhibition. Gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty (GIPP, peripheral) is autonomous production of sex steroids (or exogenous exposure) that entirely bypasses the axis — an end-organ escape. The whole diagnostic split flows from this single question: is the driver central axis activation, or autonomous peripheral steroid?
keyPoints: GDPP (central) = premature reactivation of the GnRH pulse generator; axis intact.
GIPP (peripheral) = autonomous (or exogenous) sex steroids bypassing the axis.
One question localizes everything: central activation vs autonomous peripheral steroid.

items.title: Why GDPP shows pubertal LH while GIPP shows suppressed LH
items.content: The biochemistry follows the mechanism. In GDPP the pituitary is being driven by GnRH, so LH is pubertal — either a pubertal basal LH or a brisk pubertal LH response to GnRH (or GnRH-agonist) stimulation. In GIPP the sex steroids are made autonomously, peripheral to the axis, so they feed back to suppress the hypothalamus and pituitary: LH and FSH are low and flat and do not rise with GnRH stimulation. Therefore a pubertal stimulated LH points central, while suppressed, non-responsive gonadotropins point peripheral — this single test result is the pivot of the workup.
keyPoints: GDPP: pubertal basal LH, or a pubertal LH response to GnRH stimulation.
GIPP: suppressed, flat LH/FSH that do not respond to GnRH.
Stimulated LH is the pivotal test: pubertal = central; suppressed = peripheral.

items.title: GDPP: idiopathic and constitutional causes
items.content: Most central precocious puberty in girls is idiopathic. Idiopathic/constitutional GDPP accounts for roughly 80–90% of GDPP in females and often represents the extreme early tail of the normal physiological distribution rather than a discrete lesion. In boys the picture is reversed: idiopathic GDPP is rare (under ~10%), so a central structural lesion must be aggressively ruled out in any boy with central precocious puberty. This sex difference is why a brain MRI is essentially mandatory in central precocious puberty in boys, and more selective in girls.
keyPoints: Idiopathic GDPP = ~80–90% of central precocious puberty in girls.
Often the extreme early tail of normal, not a lesion.
In boys idiopathic GDPP is rare (<~10%) → aggressively exclude a structural lesion.

items.title: Why genetic loss of the GnRH 'brake' (or kisspeptin gain) causes GDPP
items.content: Several gene defects drive central premature activation by removing inhibition or adding drive to the GnRH pulse generator. MKRN3 (makorin RING-finger protein 3) loss-of-function is the most common genetic cause of familial GDPP: MKRN3 normally exerts a fundamental inhibitory tone on GnRH secretion, so losing this 'brake' releases early-onset puberty (note the imprinted, paternal-inheritance pattern). DLK1 (delta-like non-canonical Notch ligand 1) loss-of-function similarly removes central inhibition. KISS1 / KISS1R (kisspeptin and its receptor) gain-of-function does the opposite — pathological upregulation or autonomous firing of the kisspeptin system directly accelerates GnRH pulsatility. The theme: release a brake or press an accelerator on the pulse generator.
keyPoints: MKRN3 loss of function: most common familial GDPP — releases the GnRH 'brake' (paternally inherited).
DLK1 loss of function: also removes central inhibition.
KISS1/KISS1R gain of function: accelerates GnRH pulsatility (presses the accelerator).

items.title: GDPP: CNS structural lesions
items.content: Several CNS lesions cause central precocious puberty. The hypothalamic hamartoma is the most common structural cause — ectopic neural tissue that behaves as an autonomous, ectopic GnRH pulse generator, classically associated with gelastic (laughing) seizures. Astrocytomas and gliomas, particularly optic pathway gliomas associated with Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), are important to look for. Craniopharyngiomas more often cause delayed puberty through pituitary-stalk compression but can occasionally disrupt inhibitory pathways and cause precocity. Pineal tumors are a historical association, thought to alter melatonin rhythms and remove GnRH inhibition. Any of these mandates brain imaging once central activation is confirmed.
keyPoints: Hypothalamic hamartoma: most common; ectopic GnRH generator; gelastic seizures.
Optic pathway gliomas/astrocytomas (NF1 association).
Craniopharyngioma (usually delays puberty) and pineal tumors are also implicated.

items.title: How acquired CNS insults trigger central precocious puberty
items.content: Damage to the inhibitory networks above the GnRH pulse generator can disinhibit it. Post-infectious sequelae — scarring after meningitis, encephalitis, or brain abscess — can disrupt inhibitory neural circuits and trigger central precocity. Trauma and ischemia (severe traumatic brain injury, hydrocephalus, hypoxic-ischemic events) act similarly. Iatrogenic cranial irradiation has a dose-dependent, paradoxical effect: low-dose radiation (e.g. for leukaemia) often accelerates pubertal timing by removing inhibition, whereas high-dose radiation destroys the axis and causes hypogonadism. The unifying mechanism is loss of the inhibitory tone that normally restrains the pulse generator.
keyPoints: Post-infectious scarring (meningitis/encephalitis) disrupts inhibitory circuits.
Trauma, hydrocephalus, hypoxic-ischemic injury can disinhibit the pulse generator.
Cranial irradiation: low dose accelerates puberty; high dose destroys the axis.

items.title: GIPP: gonadal causes
items.content: In peripheral precocious puberty the sex steroids come from the gonad autonomously. In girls, autonomous ovarian follicular cysts are the most common cause of GIPP — they transiently secrete high estradiol, often producing rapid breast development and a withdrawal bleed when the cyst ruptures or involutes. Ovarian tumours (granulosa cell tumours, thecomas, functioning teratomas) are rarer drivers. In boys, Leydig cell tumours secrete autonomous testosterone and typically present with asymmetric testicular enlargement, while hCG-secreting germ cell tumours drive bilateral enlargement because excess hCG cross-reacts with the LH receptor. Testicular size and symmetry therefore help localise the source.
keyPoints: Girls: autonomous ovarian follicular cyst = most common GIPP (transient estradiol, withdrawal bleed).
Ovarian tumours: granulosa cell, thecoma, functioning teratoma.
Boys: Leydig cell tumour (asymmetric testis) vs hCG germ cell tumour (bilateral via LH receptor).

items.title: GIPP: adrenal causes
items.content: The adrenal gland is a major peripheral source of androgens. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia — non-classical or late-onset classical 21-hydroxylase or 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency — shunts steroidogenesis toward the androgen pathway, driving rapid pubarche and growth. A key localising clue in boys is that adrenal androgen excess virilises without enlarging the testes (which require gonadotropin or LH-receptor drive to grow), so virilisation with prepubertal-sized testes points adrenal/CAH or exogenous, not gonadal. Adrenal tumours (virilizing or feminizing adenomas and carcinomas) are the other adrenal cause.
keyPoints: CAH (non-classical/late-onset 21-OH or 11-beta-OH) shunts to androgens → rapid pubarche/growth.
In boys: adrenal androgens virilise WITHOUT testicular enlargement (a key localiser).
Adrenal tumours: virilizing or feminizing adenomas/carcinomas.

items.title: Why McCune-Albright and testotoxicosis cause autonomous GIPP
items.content: Two activating defects lock end-organs into autonomous hormone production. McCune-Albright syndrome is caused by an activating somatic mutation in GNAS, the gene encoding the Gs-alpha protein; this produces constitutive cAMP signalling and autonomous glandular hyperfunction, with the classic triad of GIPP, café-au-lait macules ('coast of Maine', irregular borders), and polyostotic fibrous dysplasia. Familial male-limited precocious puberty (testotoxicosis) is caused by an activating mutation in LHCGR (the LH receptor): the Leydig cells are locked 'on', producing adult levels of testosterone despite suppressed LH. Both are gain-of-function lesions that switch a receptor or its G-protein permanently on.
keyPoints: McCune-Albright: activating GNAS (Gs-alpha) → constitutive cAMP; triad GIPP + café-au-lait + fibrous dysplasia.
Testotoxicosis (FMPP): activating LHCGR → Leydig cells locked on, adult testosterone with suppressed LH.
Both are gain-of-function lesions driving autonomous hormone output.

items.title: Why hypothyroidism (and ectopic hCG) can cause precocious puberty
items.content: Some GIPP arises from hormones cross-reacting with gonadal receptors. Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome is profound, long-standing primary hypothyroidism in which the massive TSH elevation — TSH shares a common alpha-subunit with FSH and LH — directly cross-reacts with and activates the FSH receptor, causing cystic ovaries, breast development, and, paradoxically, delayed bone age (because the hypothyroidism itself slows skeletal maturation). Ectopic hCG from tumours (classically hepatoblastoma in boys) stimulates the LH receptor to drive testosterone. Finally, exogenous exposure — iatrogenic or accidental topical testosterone gels, estrogen creams, or systemic anabolic steroids — produces peripheral precocity from outside the body entirely.
keyPoints: Van Wyk-Grumbach: severe primary hypothyroidism; high TSH cross-reacts with FSH receptor — note paradoxically DELAYED bone age.
Ectopic hCG (e.g. hepatoblastoma in boys) stimulates the LH receptor.
Exogenous: topical testosterone/estrogen creams, anabolic steroids.

items.title: How to localize the cause: the comparative map
items.content: Read the case against the comparative map. Node of failure: GDPP = early GnRH pulsatility; GIPP = autonomous sex-steroid excess. LH/FSH: GDPP pubertal (basal or stimulated); GIPP suppressed (prepubertal, non-responsive). Gonadal size in boys: GDPP causes bilateral testicular enlargement (≥ ~4 mL); GIPP shows prepubertal testes, asymmetric enlargement (Leydig tumour), or a tumour mass — and bilateral enlargement with suppressed LH suggests an hCG-driven or LH-receptor-activating cause. Key genetics: GDPP — MKRN3, DLK1, KISS1; GIPP — GNAS (McCune-Albright), LHCGR (testotoxicosis). Common structural drivers: GDPP — hypothalamic hamartoma, glioma; GIPP — ovarian cysts, Leydig cell tumours, adrenal masses.
keyPoints: LH/FSH: pubertal = central; suppressed/flat = peripheral.
Boys' testes: bilateral enlargement = central (or hCG/LHCGR); asymmetric = Leydig tumour; prepubertal + virilized = adrenal/exogenous.
Genetics: MKRN3/DLK1/KISS1 (central) vs GNAS/LHCGR (peripheral).

items.question: Why does a GnRH stimulation test show a pubertal LH rise in GDPP but a flat, suppressed response in GIPP?
items.answer: Because the two conditions act on opposite sides of the pituitary. In GDPP the disorder is upstream: the hypothalamic GnRH pulse generator has been prematurely reactivated and is driving the pituitary, so giving exogenous GnRH (or a GnRH agonist) elicits a brisk, pubertal LH rise — the axis is primed and responsive. In GIPP the sex steroids are produced autonomously in the periphery (gonad, adrenal, or exogenous), and that steroid excess feeds back negatively on the hypothalamus and pituitary, switching off endogenous GnRH and gonadotropins; the pituitary is therefore suppressed and does not respond to a GnRH challenge. So a pubertal stimulated LH localises the problem to a driven central axis, while a flat, suppressed LH localises it to an autonomous peripheral source — making this test the decisive fork in the workup.
keyPoints: GDPP: the pulse generator drives a primed pituitary → brisk LH rise to GnRH.
GIPP: autonomous peripheral steroids suppress the axis → flat, non-responsive LH.
The stimulated-LH result localises central vs peripheral.

items.question: How do testicular examination findings help localize the cause of precocious puberty in a boy?
items.answer: Testicular size and symmetry map onto the source of androgen. Bilateral testicular enlargement (≥ ~4 mL) indicates gonadotropin (or gonadotropin-like) drive to both testes — central GDPP, or an hCG-secreting tumour or activating LH-receptor mutation (testotoxicosis) stimulating the LH receptor bilaterally. Asymmetric or unilateral enlargement, often with a palpable mass, suggests a Leydig cell tumour producing testosterone locally on one side. Prepubertal-sized testes despite clear virilisation indicate an androgen source that does not act on the testis — adrenal androgen excess (such as CAH or an adrenal tumour) or exogenous testosterone, because those virilise without providing the gonadotropin/LH-receptor signal needed to grow the testes. Combining this with the LH/FSH result and the steroid profile usually pinpoints the cause.
keyPoints: Bilateral enlargement → central, or hCG/LH-receptor-driven.
Asymmetric/unilateral mass → Leydig cell tumour.
Prepubertal testes + virilization → adrenal (CAH/tumour) or exogenous androgen.

items.stem: What fundamentally distinguishes GDPP from GIPP?
options: GDPP is always tumoral; GIPP is always idiopathic
GDPP is premature central GnRH-driven activation (pubertal LH); GIPP is autonomous peripheral sex-steroid excess (suppressed LH)
GDPP only occurs in boys; GIPP only in girls
items.explanation: GDPP is gonadotropin-dependent — premature reactivation of the GnRH pulse generator with pubertal LH — whereas GIPP is gonadotropin-independent autonomous sex-steroid production that suppresses LH/FSH.

items.stem: What is the most common genetic cause of familial gonadotropin-dependent precocious puberty?
items.explanation: MKRN3 loss of function is the most common genetic cause of familial GDPP: MKRN3 normally inhibits GnRH secretion, so losing this brake releases early central activation (with a paternally-inherited, imprinted pattern).

items.stem: Which is the most common CNS structural lesion causing central precocious puberty, classically associated with gelastic seizures?
items.explanation: A hypothalamic hamartoma is the most common structural cause of GDPP; it behaves as an ectopic, autonomous GnRH pulse generator and is classically associated with gelastic (laughing) seizures.

items.stem: What is the most common cause of gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty in girls?
items.explanation: Autonomous ovarian follicular cysts are the most common cause of GIPP in girls, transiently secreting high estradiol and often causing rapid breast development with a withdrawal bleed on cyst involution.

items.stem: McCune-Albright syndrome is caused by which molecular defect, and what is its classic triad?
options: LHCGR loss of function; triad of short stature, deafness, cataracts
Activating somatic GNAS (Gs-alpha) mutation; triad of GIPP, café-au-lait macules, and polyostotic fibrous dysplasia
MKRN3 loss of function; triad of seizures, obesity, diabetes
21-hydroxylase deficiency; triad of salt-wasting, virilization, hypotension
items.explanation: McCune-Albright results from an activating somatic GNAS mutation causing constitutive cAMP signalling and autonomous glandular hyperfunction, with the triad of GIPP, café-au-lait macules, and polyostotic fibrous dysplasia.

items.stem: Familial male-limited precocious puberty (testotoxicosis) results from which defect?
options: An activating mutation of the LH receptor (LHCGR), locking Leydig cells 'on' with suppressed LH
items.explanation: Testotoxicosis is caused by an activating LHCGR mutation: the Leydig cells autonomously produce adult-level testosterone despite suppressed LH — a peripheral, gonadotropin-independent cause.

items.stem: In Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome, what drives the precocious puberty, and what is the characteristic bone-age finding?
options: Markedly elevated TSH cross-reacting with the FSH receptor (severe primary hypothyroidism); paradoxically DELAYED bone age
An ovarian tumour; normal bone age
items.explanation: Profound primary hypothyroidism raises TSH so much that it cross-reacts with the FSH receptor (shared alpha-subunit), causing cystic ovaries and breast development — yet the hypothyroidism itself slows skeletal maturation, so bone age is paradoxically delayed.

items.stem: A boy with precocious puberty has asymmetric (unilateral) testicular enlargement. What is the most likely cause?
options: A Leydig cell tumour producing autonomous testosterone
items.explanation: Asymmetric/unilateral testicular enlargement, often with a mass, points to a Leydig cell tumour secreting testosterone locally. Central causes and hCG-driven tumours typically enlarge both testes.

items.stem: How does cranial irradiation affect pubertal timing?
options: It always delays puberty regardless of dose
Low-dose radiation often accelerates pubertal timing, whereas high-dose radiation destroys the axis
It has no effect on the HPG axis
It only affects the gonads, not the brain
items.explanation: Cranial irradiation has a dose-dependent, paradoxical effect: low doses often disinhibit and accelerate puberty (central precocity), while high doses damage the axis and cause hypogonadism.

items.stem: A boy is clearly virilized but his testes remain prepubertal in size. Where is the androgen source most likely to be?
options: The adrenal gland (e.g. CAH or adrenal tumour) or an exogenous source
items.explanation: Virilization with prepubertal-sized testes indicates an androgen source that does not stimulate testicular growth — adrenal androgen excess (CAH or adrenal tumour) or exogenous testosterone — because testicular enlargement requires gonadotropin or LH-receptor drive.

items.statement: GDPP is characterised by a pubertal LH response to GnRH stimulation.
items.explanation: True. In central (gonadotropin-dependent) precocious puberty the primed axis produces a pubertal LH rise to GnRH stimulation.

items.statement: In GIPP, LH and FSH are suppressed and do not respond to GnRH stimulation.
items.explanation: True. Autonomous peripheral sex steroids feed back to suppress the axis, so gonadotropins are low and flat and do not rise with GnRH.

items.statement: MKRN3 normally inhibits GnRH secretion, and loss-of-function mutations cause early central puberty.
items.explanation: True. MKRN3 exerts an inhibitory 'brake' on GnRH; loss of function releases that brake and is the commonest genetic cause of familial GDPP.

items.statement: A hypothalamic hamartoma acts as an ectopic, autonomous GnRH pulse generator.
items.explanation: True. The hamartoma is ectopic neural tissue that secretes GnRH autonomously, causing central precocious puberty, often with gelastic seizures.

items.statement: McCune-Albright syndrome is caused by a loss-of-function mutation in GNAS.
items.explanation: False. It is an activating (gain-of-function) somatic GNAS mutation causing constitutive cAMP signalling and autonomous glandular hyperfunction.

items.statement: In Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome the bone age is paradoxically delayed despite the precocious puberty.
items.explanation: True. Severe primary hypothyroidism drives the precocity via TSH cross-reacting with the FSH receptor, but the hypothyroidism itself slows skeletal maturation, so bone age is delayed.

items.statement: Bilateral symmetric testicular enlargement suggests a central (or hCG/LH-receptor-driven) cause rather than a Leydig cell tumour.
items.explanation: True. Bilateral enlargement implies drive to both testes (central GnRH, hCG, or an activating LH receptor), whereas a Leydig cell tumour typically enlarges one testis asymmetrically.

items.assertion: A virilized boy with prepubertal-sized testes should be evaluated for an adrenal or exogenous androgen source.
items.reason: Adrenal androgen excess (e.g. CAH) and exogenous testosterone virilize without enlarging the testes, which require gonadotropin or LH-receptor stimulation to grow.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because testicular growth needs gonadotropin/LH-receptor drive, virilization with prepubertal testes indicates an androgen source acting outside the testis — adrenal or exogenous — which is exactly why those are the targets of evaluation.
